content.full_text:
January 7, 1977

Memo to the File

From: Ted Prim

Re: Modification of Advice to Peter Bagatelas Concerning Termination of Candidacy

I originally spoke with Peter Bagatelas of Dobbs, Doyle and Nielson on November 24, 1976. At that time I advised him that a person who has been defeated in an election campaign and who does not intend to seek office in the future ceases to be a candidate after the closing date for his post-election statement has past. However, Commissioner Quinn asked that this issue be reconsidered. Accordingly, the issue was reevaluated at the Opinion Request meeting of December 31, 1976. It was the decision of that group that our previous advice be modified as follows:

A person becomes a candidate whenever he receives contributions or makes expenditures with view to seeking an elective office regardless of whether the office sought is known at the time. Once a person becomes a candidate, he remains a candidate until he permanently ceases to raise or expend funds. Thus, a candidate with a campaign debt must file semi-annual statements pursuant to Section 84206 whenever he receives contributions or makes expenditures for the purpose of reducing his debt. If a candidate does not receive any contributions or make any expenditures during a period, he need not file.

We also concluded that the prohibitions against a lobbyist making or arranging for a contribution remain in effect as long as the potential recipient continues to be a candidate regardless of whether he is filing reports. See Sections 86200, 86202. However, the staff was divided as to whether the gift prohibition applied after a defeated candidate filed his post-election statement. Accordingly, I advised Mr. Bagatelos that to be safe his client should also observe the gift prohibitions until, by all definitions, the person was no longer a candidate.

I provided this advice to Mr. Bagatelos on December 31, the 58th day after the November election. He told me that he thought he could convey this latest advice to his clients before any of them took action pursuant to the previous advice. I told him to call me back immediately if he found that gifts or contributions had been made before he was able to communicate with his client.

sections.conclusion: A person becomes a candidate whenever he receives contributions or makes expenditures with view to seeking an elective office regardless of whether the office sought is known at the time. Once a person becomes a candidate, he remains a candidate until he permanently ceases to raise or expend funds. Thus, a candidate with a campaign debt must file semi-annual statements pursuant to Section 84206 whenever he receives contributions or makes expenditures for the purpose of reducing his debt. If a candidate does not receive any contributions or make any expenditures during a period, he need not file. The prohibitions against a lobbyist making or arranging for a contribution remain in effect as long as the potential recipient continues to be a candidate regardless of whether he is filing reports. The gift prohibition should also be observed until, by all definitions, the person was no longer a candidate.
sections.question_synthetic: When does a person cease to be a candidate for purposes of FPPC reporting requirements and lobbyist contribution prohibitions, particularly following a defeated election campaign?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC modified its prior advice to clarify that a defeated candidate remains a candidate and subject to reporting and lobbyist contribution prohibitions until he permanently ceases raising or expending funds. A candidate with campaign debt must file semi-annual statements when receiving contributions or making expenditures to reduce that debt, and lobbyist contribution prohibitions apply regardless of filing status.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Internal memorandum documenting modification of advice based on Commissioner Quinn's request for reconsideration. No formal Q/A sections present; conclusion extracted from body text. Staff was divided on gift prohibition application post-election statement filing, creating some ambiguity in final advice.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: When does a person cease to be a candidate for purposes of FPPC reporting requirements and lobbyist contribution prohibitions, particularly following a defeated election campaign?

CONCLUSION: A person becomes a candidate whenever he receives contributions or makes expenditures with view to seeking an elective office regardless of whether the office sought is known at the time. Once a person becomes a candidate, he remains a candidate until he permanently ceases to raise or expend funds. Thus, a candidate with a campaign debt must file semi-annual statements pursuant to Section 84206 whenever he receives contributions or makes expenditures for the purpose of reducing his debt. If a candidate does not receive any contributions or make any expenditures during a period, he need not file. The prohibitions against a lobbyist making or arranging for a contribution remain in effect as long as the potential recipient continues to be a candidate regardless of whether he is filing reports. The gift prohibition should also be observed until, by all definitions, the person was no longer a candidate.

embedding.summary: This is an internal FPPC memo modifying prior advice given to Peter Bagatelas regarding when a defeated candidate ceases to be a candidate under California law. The modification clarifies that candidates remain subject to reporting requirements and lobbyist contribution prohibitions until they permanently stop raising and expending funds.
